sections.title: What it is
sections.content: On stainless steel, cooked-on grease typically affects the passive oxide layer or sits as a film above it. A visible condition that changes how refrigerator shelves looks or feels under normal light. Grain direction and micro-scratches change how light catches the defect.

sections.title: Why it happens
sections.content: Chlorides, acidic foods, and abrasive pads disturb the protective layer so cooked-on grease shows faster. Cooking oils and aerosolized fat resettle and oxidize, so bond strength increases with heat and time. Wrong-grain scrubbing creates micro-grooves that trap soil.

sections.title: Where it appears
sections.content: Around sinks, welded seams, and horizontal rails—cooked-on grease on refrigerator shelves follows rinse patterns and where dissimilar metals touch.

sections.title: How to fix it
sections.content: On stainless steel, cooked-on grease sits on the surface layer. Use a non-abrasive cleaner, wipe in the direction of the grain, and avoid circular scrubbing to prevent scratching. Rinse completely so chlorides do not dry on the surface.

sections.title: What to avoid
sections.content: Do not use steel wool, chlorine bleach baths, or scrubbing across the grain—each can embed rust-prone particles or strip the passive layer while fighting cooked-on grease.

sections.title: What to expect
sections.content: Depending on severity, removing cooked-on grease from refrigerator shelves may take multiple passes, drying intervals, and material-specific follow-up. Stop if gloss drops or texture changes—reassess chemistry before forcing another round.

snapshots.title: What Causes cooked-on grease on shower grout

sections.title: What it is
sections.content: In grout lines, cooked-on grease concentrates in low, porous cementitious channels that trap moisture and soil. A visible condition that changes how shower grout looks or feels under normal light. The contrast against tile makes even slight discoloration obvious.

sections.title: Why it happens
sections.content: Residual soap, minerals, and body soils thin-spread across tile but drain into grout joints where cooked-on grease concentrates. Cooking oils and aerosolized fat resettle and oxidize, so bond strength increases with heat and time. Lower spots stay wet longest.

sections.title: Where it appears
sections.content: Corners, curb lines, and niche bottoms hold water longest—expect cooked-on grease on shower grout to start there before the field grout looks bad.

sections.title: How to fix it
sections.content: Target grout joints with a narrow brush and a cleaner rated for cementitious grout; let chemistry loosen cooked-on grease before mechanical work. Avoid sawing motions that gouge grout; rinse and dry joints so moisture does not re-wick pigment.

sections.title: What to avoid
sections.content: Avoid one-size-fits-all “maximum strength” products that ignore shower grout limits; Skip random acids on colored mortar and unknown stone thresholds nearby. Test hidden areas before full passes.

sections.title: What to expect
sections.content: First pass often clears tile face while cooked-on grease in grout still reads darker; second passes on joints complete the job. Uniform color returns after full dry—wet grout always looks worse temporarily.

snapshots.title: What Causes cooked-on grease on chrome fixtures

sections.title: What it is
sections.content: On stainless steel, cooked-on grease typically affects the passive oxide layer or sits as a film above it. A visible condition that changes how chrome fixtures looks or feels under normal light. Grain direction and micro-scratches change how light catches the defect.

sections.title: Why it happens
sections.content: Chlorides, acidic foods, and abrasive pads disturb the protective layer so cooked-on grease shows faster. Cooking oils and aerosolized fat resettle and oxidize, so bond strength increases with heat and time. Wrong-grain scrubbing creates micro-grooves that trap soil.

sections.title: Where it appears
sections.content: Around sinks, welded seams, and horizontal rails—cooked-on grease on chrome fixtures follows rinse patterns and where dissimilar metals touch.

sections.title: How to fix it
sections.content: On stainless steel, cooked-on grease sits on the surface layer. Use a non-abrasive cleaner, wipe in the direction of the grain, and avoid circular scrubbing to prevent scratching. Rinse completely so chlorides do not dry on the surface.

sections.title: What to avoid
sections.content: Do not use steel wool, chlorine bleach baths, or scrubbing across the grain—each can embed rust-prone particles or strip the passive layer while fighting cooked-on grease.

sections.title: What to expect
sections.content: Depending on severity, removing cooked-on grease from chrome fixtures may take multiple passes, drying intervals, and material-specific follow-up. Stop if gloss drops or texture changes—reassess chemistry before forcing another round.
